text: Token Economics (Tokconomics) Jensen Huang's framework (GTC26): computers are no longer tools, they are manufacturing equipment. Like ASML lithography machines or dynamos, they produce something (tokens) that is sold. The economics of AI infrastructure should be evaluated as factory economics, not IT procurement. Core Insight The price of the computer and... # Token Economics (Tokconomics) Jensen Huang's framework (GTC26): computers are no longer tools, they are manufacturing equipment. Like ASML lithography machines or dynamos, they produce something (tokens) that is sold. The economics of AI infrastructure should be evaluated as factory economics, not IT procurement. ## Core Insight The price of the computer and the cost of the token are "only marginally related." What matters is **tokens/second/watt** — production efficiency of the factory. - Nvidia delivers enough value gap that c...

text: Revenue +168% YoY, backlog 4x'd to $66.8B, 'unable to catch up with demand signals', all capacity booked through Aug-Sep 2026
title: CRWV

text: Delivered 50K GB200s in Q4 to single customer. Nvidia GPUs 'remain in short supply' per Intrator. Massive capex plan ($30-35B) = enormous chip demand.
title: CRWV

text: Big tech total 2026 AI spending estimated around $650B, +80% YoY; Google and Amazon remain the two largest incremental demand engines for accelerator clusters; Cloud procurement cadence supports sustained NVDA and CRWV order visibility
title: Hyperscaler CapEx Pull Through

text: Rubin moving to N3, competing with TPU v7/v8, Trainium3, MI350X for TSMC capacity. $95.2B supply commitments (nearly doubled QoQ) = enormous wafer demand. Vera Rubin production H2 2026 adds further N3 pressure. Six new chips this cycle — all need N3.
title: NVDA

text: Supply commitments doubled ($50.3B → $95.2B). $500B revenue visibility. Demand "growing exponentially." Validates CRWV thesis — NVIDIA can't build fast enough.
title: NVDA

text: Q4 revenue reached $33.73B with gross margin 62.3%, and management said both Q4 and Q1 2026 were supported by strong demand for leading-edge process technologies.
title: TSM

text: 3nm already accounted for 28% of wafer revenue, and 7nm-and-below advanced nodes were 77% of total wafer revenue, showing AI demand remains concentrated in constrained leading-edge supply.
title: TSM

text: A 2026 capital budget of $52B-$56B is a real supply response. It does not break the shortage today, but it is exactly the kind of capex that can eventually ease logic tightness.
title: TSM

text: AI = 60% of TSMC N3 output (2026), projected 86% by 2027; Reallocating 5% smartphone N3 wafers = ~100K Rubin GPUs or ~300K TPU v7s; Google 2026 capex roughly doubled vs prior expectations; Amazon 2026 AI infrastructure spend remains on a steep expansion path; All major AI accelerators moving to N3 simultaneously
title: The Great AI Silicon Shortage

text: H100 1yr rental: $1.70/hr (Oct 2025) → $2.35/hr (Mar 2026), +40%; AWS p6-b200 spot: $14/hr/GPU; Month-on-month rental increases 15-20% (Jan-Mar 2026); All new capacity through Aug-Sep 2026 already booked; Blackwell deployment lead times extended to Jun-Jul 2026
title: The Great GPU Shortage — Rental Capacity

text: ASML tooling cadence remains the long-run upper bound for advanced wafer output.
